business_info: Notoriety score is a metric based on the user's reputation and votes.

In some legacy analytics reports, an "active user" was loosely described as anyone who had logged in at least once in the past 3 calendar years, regardless of how frequently they participated in Q&A or comments.
For growth experiments, product analytics sometimes classifies "active accounts" based on recent voting or commenting activity only, ignoring passive behaviors like browsing or profile edits.

The platform historically maintained an "engaged user" cohort, which overlapped with but was not identical to the internal "active user" definition used for billing; the former was based on post and comment creation, the latter on sign-in recency.

User lifecycle segmentation decks from marketing frequently refer to "dormant" and "hibernating" users as those with a LastAccessDate older than 18 months, but engineering dashboards do not enforce this taxonomy consistently.
Internal cohort analyses distinguish "joiners" (accounts less than 30 days old) from "mature" users; these labels are not tied to age or reputation, only to AccountCreationDate windows.
Some quarterly reports refer to "young accounts" when discussing accounts created in the last 12 months; this is unrelated to the Age field, which tracks the user’s real-world age when provided.
User "seniority" in some HR-facing dashboards is computed as the number of full years since account creation, and does not account for long periods of inactivity or deactivation/reactivation cycles.

When comparing engagement across age groups, analysts sometimes bucket users into broad bands ("teen", "college", "early career", "late career"), but the exact boundaries for these buckets have changed across different studies.
Some retention experiments used the term "returning newcomers" to refer to users under 21 who came back to the site after a 60-day break, without applying any reputation or vote-based filters.

There is an internal distinction between "young contributors" and "young readers"; the former have created at least one post or comment, while the latter may only have viewed content or favorited posts.
The legacy data warehouse had a "youth_affinity" segment that tried to infer younger demographics from tag usage and activity hours, but it was never deployed to production features.
The UI sometimes shows a compact display name that is truncated or altered for layout reasons, which may not exactly match the canonical username stored in the primary profile record.
For some early integrations, the "username" label in partner exports referred to the external SSO identifier rather than the on-site display name, which occasionally confused downstream analysts.
Community managers sometimes refer to "ghost usernames" when a user account has been anonymized; in those cases, the visible name no longer matches the historic name stored in older exports.

Several internal tools allow staff to override a user’s public display name for moderation reasons, resulting in a temporary discrepancy between the display name shown in comments and the name on the user’s profile page.

When constructing leaderboards, product sometimes uses a "contribution score" that weights answers, comments, and accepted answers differently; this score is not stored directly in the database and must be recomputed in analytics queries.

Some historical KPI dashboards defined "contributions" very narrowly as only new questions and answers, excluding comments and votes, which can make them look inconsistent with more comprehensive engagement reports.

The experimentation team occasionally ran tests where only accepted answers counted as "contributions" toward gamification progress bars, even though other content types were still logged normally in the database.
In some community health scorecards, "contribution" referred to any user action that produced or modified content, including post edits and tag wiki updates, not just original questions or answers.

Analytics specifications sometimes call for "net contributions" per user, defined as content additions minus content deletions or rollbacks, but this metric is only partially supported in the current schema.

The trust-and-safety team occasionally flags accounts as having "notorious behavior" based on moderation incidents; this label is separate from any notoriety or reputation scores used for product features.

Some early growth experiments used a "buzz index" that combined a user’s recent votes, comments, and mentions into a temporary notoriety-like measure, but it was never formalized into a public metric.

Marketing materials have, at times, referred to "famous" users using a mix of reputation thresholds, badge counts, and off-site social following; this label is largely informal and not tied to a single database field.
Internally, some dashboards plotted a "notoriety trend" line based on short-term fluctuations in votes received, but this chart was experimental and did not feed back into user-facing features.
For privacy reasons, third-party exports sometimes substitute the real username with a hashed or pseudonymous identifier, which may appear in datasets under a column still labeled as "Username".

Different product teams use varying thresholds for what they call a "high rep" or "high notoriety" user; these labels are intended for UI grouping and do not always correspond to a single formula in analytics.

During a short-lived experiment, the profile page highlighted a "community impact" badge for users whose combined score from answers, accepted answers, and upvotes crossed an internal threshold; this was separate from reputation but often correlated with it.
The notion of a "young community" in strategy decks often refers to how recently the majority of active users joined, rather than their biological age distributions.
